Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia28]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia28]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]]

B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[category:Mountain faith]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[category:Musashi Province]] [[category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]]

Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:金鑚神社]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]]

It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another category]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:金鑚神社]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}}

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another Category]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:金鑚神社]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:qq-]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]]

A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another Category]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:金鑚神社]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:qq-]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category: :qq]]

Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another Category]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:金鑚神社]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:qq-]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Completion tier 5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]]

Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to aqq another Category]] [[Category:qq]][[ja:金鑚神社]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Cqqqqqqqqq5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Cqqqqqqqqq5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to aqq another Category]] [[Category:qq]][[ja:金鑚神社]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Cqqqqqqqqq5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 1]]\[[Category:Cqqqqqqqqq5]][[Category:tier 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:qq 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]]

It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another Category]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:金鑚神社]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category: 5]][[Category:dd 1]]\[[Category: 5]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

[[Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category: 5]][[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another Category]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:金鑚神社]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category: 5]][[Category:dd 1]]\[[Category: 5]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:5]][[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:5]][[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another Category]] [[Category:dd]][[ja:金鑚神社]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:5]][[Category:dd 1]]\[[Category:5]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

[[Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:5]] [[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another Category]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]]

B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:bgoodbye $1]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:5]] [[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:tab went inactive so I need to add another Category]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:5]] [[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd 1]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kanasana Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]]
