It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:5]] [[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:5]] [[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]]

Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:dd 5]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ambox transclusions]] {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]]

It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]]

Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:二宮]] [[Category:五宮]] [[Category:埼玉県の重要文化財]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:二宮]] [[Category:五宮]] [[Category:埼玉県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]]

B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:二宮]] [[Category:五宮]] [[Category:埼玉県の重要文化財]] [[Category:室町時代の建築]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:神川町の建築物]] [[Category:神川町の歴史]] [[Category:重要文化財 (建造物)]]

It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:二宮]] [[Category:五宮]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:神川町の建築物]] [[Category:神川町の歴史]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]]

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:dd 1]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:二宮]] [[Category:五宮]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:神川町の建築物]] [[Category:神川町の歴史]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:五宮]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:神川町の建築物]] [[Category:神川町の歴史]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:五宮]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:神川町の建築物]] [[Category:神川町の歴史]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:五宮]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:神川町の建築物]] [[Category:神川町の歴史]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:五宮]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:神川町の建築物]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:五宮]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:五宮]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

{{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[:en:Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[:en:Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:qq]]

B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[:en:Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]

Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]
