revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanasana-jinja keidai.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kamikawa|qid=Q1346757}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.180256|N|139.073061|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[:en:Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]]

It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[sn:Kanasana Shrine]] [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Kanasana-jinja]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[ja:金鑚神社]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanasana-jinja keidai.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kamikawa|qid=Q1346757}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.180256|N|139.073061|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[:en:Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}}

Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. [[sn:commons:Category:Kanasana-jinja]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[ja:金鑚神社]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanasana-jinja keidai.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kamikawa|WD=Q1346757}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.180256|N|139.073061|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[:en:Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] {{wikidata link|Q1}} {{wikidata link|Q1}}

Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]] {{wikidata link|Q1}} {{wikidata link|Q1}} {{wikidata link|Q1}} {{wikidata link|Q1}} {{wikidata link|Q1}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Kanasana-jinja]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[ja:金鑚神社]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanasana-jinja keidai.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kamikawa|WD=Q1346757}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.180256|N|139.073061|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[:en:Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[:en:Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[:en:Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]]

It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]]

[[co:commons:Category:Kanasana-jinja]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[ja:金鑚神社]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[co:commons:Category:Kanasana-jinja]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[ja:金鑚神社]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[co:commons:Category:Kanasana-jinja]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[ja:金鑚神社]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[sn:commons:Category:Kanasana-jinja]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[ja:金鑚神社]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanasana-jinja keidai.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kamikawa|WD=Q1346757}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.180256|N|139.073061|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa, Saitama]].{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[:en:Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of [[:en:Musashi Province|Musashi Province]], or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama Prefecture]].{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] hid the tools of fire in [[:en:Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|Mount Ōmuro]]. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in [[:en:Kamikawa, Saitama|Kamikawa]], in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between [[:en:Saitama Prefecture|Saitama]] and [[:en:Gunma Prefecture|Gunma prefectures]]. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality [[:en:Ironsand|iron sand]] from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] [[:en:Camphor|Camphor]] tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside [[:en:Ōmiwa Shrine|Ōmiwa Shrine]] and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive [[:en:Slickenside|slickenslide]], a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]]

It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}} and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive {{ill|Slickenside|en|Slickenside|WD=Q478099|lt=slickenslide}}, a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Kanasana-jinja]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[ja:金鑚神社]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[co:commons:Category:Kanasana-jinja]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[ja:金鑚神社]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[co:commons:Category:Kanasana-jinja]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[ja:金鑚神社]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] [[sn:commons:Category:Kanasana-jinja]][[en:Kanasana Shrine]][[ja:金鑚神社]][[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Kanasana-jinja keidai.JPG|prefecture={{ill|Kamikawa|WD=Q1346757}}|coordinates={{Coord|36.180256|N|139.073061|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Amaterasu|WD=Q455602}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:21, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11647974]] Wikidata name: Kanasana Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto Shrine in Saitama}} {{Expand Japanese|金鑚神社}} {{infobox Shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Kanasana-jinja_keidai.JPG | deity = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kanasana Shrine'''|Kanasana Jinja|金鑚神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Kamikawa, Saitama|en|Kamikawa, Saitama|WD=Q1346757|lt=Kamikawa, Saitama}}.{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=nippon.com |language=en}}{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-dd |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |pages=383 |language=English}} The shrine worships [[Amaterasu]] alongside [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]. It is a mid-range Ranked Imperial shrine ([[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]), and is a [[:en:Ninomiya (shrine)|Ninomiya]] of {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}, or a second Rank shrine in the province after the [[Ichinomiya]], and is commonly called "Ninomiya-sama". It is considered a prestigious shrines in the {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama Prefecture}}.{{Cite web |title=Kanasana Shrine｜CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |url=https://en.chocotabi-saitama.com/spot/34609 |access-date=2023-10-01 |website=CHOCOTABI SAITAMA |language=ja}} The shrine is believed to have been founded when {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} hid the tools of fire in {{ill|Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|en|Mount Ōmuro (Mount Fuji)|WD=Q11434369|lt=Mount Ōmuro}}. Every November 23 it hosts a {{Nihongo|firestone festival|火金鑚祭|Hikiri Shinji}}{{Cite web |title=Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms: K |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}} == Overview == Kanasana Shrine is in {{ill|Kamikawa, Saitama|en|Kamikawa, Saitama|WD=Q1346757|lt=Kamikawa}}, in the northwest of Saitama Prefecture, at an elevation of 343 meters at the base of Mount Mitake. The shrine overlooks the Kannagawa river, which demarcates the border between {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|en|Saitama Prefecture|WD=Q128186|lt=Saitama}} and {{ill|Gunma Prefecture|en|Gunma Prefecture|WD=Q129499|lt=Gunma prefectures}}. According to legend, following his achievements in the eastern regions, Prince {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} placed a ''hikirigane'' (a metal instrument used for kindling fire) here as a tribute to the revered deities, [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] and [[Amaterasu]]. == Etymology == The name "Kanasana" originates from the term for "iron sand," highlighting the high-quality {{ill|Ironsand|en|Ironsand|WD=Q1394005|lt=iron sand}} from the Kannagawa River used in traditional sword making. Mount Mitake is steeped in mythology, with legends indicating the presence of iron and copper deposits. Archaeological evidence suggests human settlement in the area dating back between 15,000 and 20,000 years (to the [[:en:Jomon period|Jomon period]], likely for metal mining. == Architecture == The shrine has a large [[shinboku]] {{ill|Camphor|en|Camphor|WD=Q181559|lt=Camphor}} tree, and includes a [[Tahōtō|tahōtō pagoda]] dated from 1534{{Cite web |date=2018-07-08 |title=Keeping Ancient Mountain Worship Alive at Saitama's Kanasana Shrine |url=https://www.nippon.com/en/guide-to-japan/gu009002/keeping-ancient-mountain-worship-alive-at-saitama%E2%80%99s-kanasana-shrine.html |access-date=2023-10-18 |website=nippon.com |language=en}} The shrine omits a [[Honden]] (main hall) as the mountain behind it is considered the shrine's [[Shintai]]. It is one of only three major shrines that continue the practice of direct [[:en:Mountain Worship|Mountain Worship]] via [[Kannabi]] alongside {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|WD=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine}} and [[Suwa-taisha]].{{Cite book |last1=Cali |first1=Joseph |url=https://muse.jhu.edu/pub/5/monograph/book/21618 |title=Shinto Shrines: A Guide to the Sacred Sites of Japan's Ancient Religion |last2=Dougill |first2=John |date=2012 |publisher=University of Hawai'i Press |isbn=978-0-8248-3775-4 |location=Honolulu |pages=195}} In this case the [[Kannabi]] is Mimurogatake, a visible peak of Mount Mitake from the shrine's worship hall.[[File:Kanasana Jinjya Tahōtō 2009.jpg|thumb|Tahoto]] [[File:Mitake no Kagamiiwa Slickenside rock surface.jpg|thumb|Mitake no Kagami Iwa]] Above the shrine is an expansive {{ill|Slickenside|en|Slickenside|WD=Q478099|lt=slickenslide}}, a smooth rock surface spanning 9 meters with a 4-meter height, showcases a mirrored finish. Scientists attribute its sheen to the intense friction resulting from the Hachiōji tectonic line's emergence around 100 million years ago. Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]]

Recognized for its geological significance, this rock was designated a national special natural monument in 1956. == References == {{reflist}}{{Atsuta Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{translated page|ja|金鑚神社|version=101666740|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[en:Kanasana Shrine]] [[ja:金鑚神社]] [[ja:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{Commons category|Kanasana-jinja}} [[simple:Kanasana Shrine]] {{wikidata link|Q11647974}} [[Category:Architecture of Kamikawa Town]] [[Category:Architecture of Muromachi period]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Gomiya]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Saitama prefecture]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Mountain faith]] [[Category:Municipal History of Kamikawa]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Saitama Prefecture]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed parentheses]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Senior Sixth Rank]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]]
